title: S&P 500 Annual Returns by Year (1928-2026)
description: Complete S&P 500 returns by year (1928-2026). Analyze historical stock market performance, inflation-adjusted returns, and decade averages.

schemas.headline: S&P 500 Annual Returns by Year (1928-2026)
schemas.description: Complete S&P 500 returns by year (1928-2026). Analyze historical stock market performance, inflation-adjusted returns, and decade averages.

mainEntity.name: What is the average S&P 500 return per year?

acceptedAnswer.text: The historical average annual return of the S&P 500 is approximately 10% before inflation, and around 7% when adjusted for inflation, assuming dividends are reinvested.

mainEntity.name: What was the worst year for the S&P 500?

acceptedAnswer.text: The worst year for the S&P 500 was 1931, when the market fell 43.34% during the Great Depression. In recent history, 2008 was the worst year with a decline of 38.49%.

mainEntity.name: How often does the S&P 500 have a negative year?

acceptedAnswer.text: Historically, the S&P 500 has a negative annual return roughly once every four years, meaning it is positive about 73% of the time.

mainEntity.name: Does the S&P 500 return include dividends?

acceptedAnswer.text: The standard quoted S&P 500 price return does not include dividends. However, the S&P 500 Total Return index, which is often cited for long-term performance, includes the reinvestment of all dividends.

mainEntity.name: What is the inflation-adjusted return of the S&P 500?

acceptedAnswer.text: The inflation-adjusted (or real) return of the S&P 500 is the total return minus the inflation rate. Over the long term, the real return of the S&P 500 has averaged around 6.5% to 7% annualized.
